And because blaming meets our needs, we protect our right to blame. And we protect our right to blame because very often someone actually did something to hurt us. So what people tend to do is create a generalization saying like, oh, you didn't care. You didn't pay attention. No one helped me. I was crying out. ## [18:20 - 21:29]

This is one of the techniques we use when working with couples who have a pattern of saying things that set them way back. So for example, they'll be experiencing a lot of new growth and love in their relationship for many weeks and then they fall into the same old pattern where they have a huge fight where usually one partner feels they need to share all of their worries and complaints and they stack everything. And then the couple feels that they're back at level zero again, needing to build up. So one thing we ask clients is that both of them decide that any bad conversation can have a redo so that you can always say to your husband or your wife or your child can say to you or your best friend, whoever the two people are can say, I need a redo and that these are the steps of the redo to state your higher intention, to consider what you needed to say differently, and to restate your intention and say what you really meant or let the person know you will come to them in the future. It doesn't matter. You just want them to know your highest higher purpose in communicating with them that you show them your love or your respect or whatever it is. Yeah. And I think what's important here also is when they revisit that conversation that hurts, you want to go back to what you really meant to say, like reset the intention, but you don't want to get into a situation where you're saying, well, you had just said this and so you don't want to get into a bean counting conversation where they start rehashing like the point system of who did what wrong. So going back and saying, you know, this is how I was feeling or this is how I was reacting and this is what I really mean now. Sometimes people will go backwards into, well, you had just said this and that pissed me off from that point of so much. That's how you redo that conversation. Right. And a more advanced use of this kind of strategy that I just used with a woman who has this type of pattern with her husband is to ask your client to actually buy a large piece of paper, a large colorful board where they write what their overall highest intent is for their relationship. And when their partner triggers them and tries to have a fight, they say, yes, honey, I will discuss this with you, but I need my board. And they find the board and then they just read what they've already written there is their purpose and the relationship. And they actually already have little notes of what the, how the conversation usually goes, what the test is. So they're prepared and they don't, they don't stop their spouse from talking to them.

## [33:48 - 36:49]

that kind of family intervention applies to any situation where you have adults that are in trouble and you want to use the support or the resources of the family. And so it's a very different kind of interview from when there are children in the family and the children have issues. And then he does the same with what he and his mother, because he didn't want the mother to feel left aside. And so the principle to take home from this, to apply to your coaching practice, is that when you're working in a family situation or with a couple where you have to talk about the harsh realities and the financial situation, you immediately switch to love or to spirituality to end the session at a higher note at a higher level. And of course, love is universal to everyone. With some people you can talk about something spiritual without you can't, but about love you can talk with everyone. ## [36:49 - 39:53]

And it's in many ways preparing Joaquin for what will be expected of him next when he has to meet with the Australian coach, which of course Joaquin doesn't know that this is going to happen. So I just want to point out some of the pros and wisdom of the coach that anybody can use in their coaching practice and that Tony is going to appeal to afterwards when they talk with the Australian coach. So the idea that women is doing their best is not really about women. The same with success. It has to do with doing your best. The idea not to let your happiness depend on things that you cannot control. Accepting the reality of the way things are. The idea that success is peace of mind. I think that is a great one. To accomplish peace of mind is what success truly is. And then to strive for perfection even though we know that we will never reach it. And then another very interesting one is that an activity can give you joy but it's not your life. There's no activity that we present during entire life. It's just something that can give you joy. And then when he says that love is the most important word in the language and after love the word is giving. And the fact that love is in there until you give it away. I thought that those were wonderful statements. So then it was interesting to me how he set up the interview with the Australian coach. And it can be, you can consider that interview as similar, not only to any family negotiation where there has been a conflict between two or more people, but to any business negotiation also, when there's a conflict between partners or a conflict in the hierarchy where someone is in a superior position to somebody else. One interesting thing from the point of view of families and coaching families is that some people, and I wanted to mention this at the beginning that I forgot, some people prefer to interview every single family member individually first to make a connection and then bring everybody together only when the coach has made a connection with each individual already and they trust him. It's a different way of working but it's an interesting way of working that applies to very difficult situations and very difficult conflicts. Chloe, could I ask you a question about your own coaching work?

## [39:53 - 43:02]

When you sometimes go to a family to work with them intensely, a big family, is that usually what you do? You meet with each person individually? No. No? No. But sometimes I have, well, yes, for example, with a couple when there are many serious issues in a couple, I would speak on the phone with each privately first, or with a couple when I'm trying to prevent the board, I will bring them in to say hello and get acquainted together and then I will say, now I'm going to talk with only the husband. And so I will call you on your cell phone when we're ready and then we'll change places and I'll speak only with the wife. So I've done that in situations of intense conflict and also in business coaching, speaking with people individually. But you know, at least the one that was very interesting, one time I asked Jay Kaylee, who was a famous family therapist, if you were to do therapy yourself instead of teaching it, if you were to do a family therapy yourself because he only taught, how would he do it? And he said, oh, I would do it completely differently from what I teach. I would interview everybody individually before I bring him to the other.
